1628	  % `infer_instance`. The GR-glue lane is CLOSED; next GR steps are
1629	  % \cref{lem:gr_separated} / \cref{lem:gr_proper}.
1630	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
1631	  % affine patches" (read from
1632	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
1633	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1634	  % "Therefore the schemes
1635	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
1636	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
1637	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
1638	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
1639	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
1640	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
1641	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
1642	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1643	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
1644	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
1645	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
1646	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
1647	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
1648	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
1649	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
1650	  \[
1651	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
1652	  \]
1653	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
1654	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
1655	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
1656	
1657	  \emph{Construction of the glue datum.} The gluing is carried out through the
1658	  Mathlib vehicle for scheme gluing, a glue datum in the sense of a category of
1659	  schemes (the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\), extending the abstract
1660	  glue-datum interface). Its data are supplied as follows. The index set is the
1661	  set \(J := \{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\)
1662	  subsets. The objects are the charts \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}); the
1663	  pairwise overlaps are the schemes \(U^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}); the
1664	  inclusions \(f_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^I\) are the open immersions
1665	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}\) (open immersions by
1666	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}, in particular monomorphisms), with the
1667	  identity field \(f_{\mathrm{id}}\) supplied by
1668	  \cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_self_isIso}; the transition isomorphisms
1669	  \(t_{I,J} : U^I_J \to U^J_I\) are the scheme-level transitions
1670	  \(\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}\), with identity field \(t_{\mathrm{id}}\)
1671	  supplied by \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}.
1672	
1673	  The triple-overlap field \(t'\) and its first coherence obligation are supplied
1674	  by the two preceding results. The field itself is the morphism
1675	  \(t'_{I,J,K} : U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K \to U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\) of
1676	  \cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}, and the compatibility field
1677	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}}\) -- that \(t'\) is compatible with the projections to the
1678	  charts -- is \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}, which reduces (both fibre
1679	  products being affine) to the ring identity
1680	  \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_ringIdentity} via the leg lemmas
1681	  \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_fst} and \cref{lem:gr_awayPullbackIso_inv_snd}.
1682	
1683	  \emph{Cocycle field.} The \(t'\)-cocycle coherence
1684	  \(t'_{I,J,K} \circ t'_{J,K,I} \circ t'_{K,I,J} = \mathrm{id}\)
1685	  follows from \cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_cocycle} via the ring identity
1686	  \cref{lem:gr_cocycle_phi_id}. With this field in hand the glue datum is
1687	  complete: it is the structure \(\mathtt{Scheme.GlueData}\) with
1688	  \(U := \texttt{affineChart}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}),
1689	  \(V := \texttt{chartOverlap}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}),
1690	  \(f := \texttt{chartIncl}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_incl}),
1691	  \(t := \texttt{chartTransition}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition}),
1692	  \(t_{\mathrm{id}} := \texttt{chartTransition\_self}\)
1693	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition_self}),
1694	  \(t' := \texttt{chartTransition'}\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}),
1695	  \(t_{\mathrm{fac}} := \texttt{chartTransition'\_fac}\)
1696	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartTransition'_fac}), and the cocycle field above. The glued
1697	  scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is then the colimit
1698	  \((\text{glue datum}).\mathrm{glued}\) produced by the gluing machine.
1699	
1700	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
1701	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
1702	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
1703	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
1704	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
1705	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
1706	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
1707	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
1708	  follows.
1709	\end{definition}
1710	
1711	\section{Separatedness}
1712	\label{sec:gr_separated}
1713	
1714	The surjectivity of the restricted-diagonal comorphism is the ring-theoretic
1715	heart of separatedness. We isolate it through the following sequence of
1716	helper results before stating the keystone lemma.
1717	
1718	\begin{lemma}
1719	[Two-sided inverse identity \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\,P^I_J = 1\)]
1720	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1721	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1722	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1723	  In \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the pre-localisation transition hom
1724	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies the explicit
1725	  two-sided inverse identity
1726	  \[
1727	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_I\bigr) \cdot P^I_J \;=\; 1 .
1728	  \]
1729	  This refines the unit assertion of \cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit} to a named
1730	  inverse: \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the explicit inverse of \(P^I_J\) in
1731	  \(R^I_J\).
1732	\end{lemma}
1733	\begin{proof}
1734	  \uses{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap, lem:gr_transition_pre_unit, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}
1735	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet} (taking the third subset \(K = I\)),
1736	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\) is the determinant of the \(I\)-minor of the image
1737	  matrix \(M = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\), which by
1738	  \cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap} equals
1739	  \(\det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I\bigr) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\). Hence
1740	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\), and multiplying by \(P^I_J =
1741	  \det(X^I_J)\) gives \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1} \cdot \det(X^I_J) = 1\) by the
1742	  localisation-inverse cancellation for the unit \(\det(X^I_J)\)
1743	  (\cref{lem:gr_transition_pre_unit}).
1744	\end{proof}
1745	
1746	\begin{definition}
1747	[Restricted-diagonal comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1748	  \label{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap}
1750	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1751	  The \emph{restricted-diagonal comorphism} on the patch \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
1752	  U^J\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
1753	  \[
1754	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
1755	    \longrightarrow R^I_J,
1756	    \qquad
1757	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad
1758	    1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
1759	  \]
1760	  whose surjectivity is the content of separatedness; this is the \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1761	  of \cref{lem:gr_separated}.
1762	\end{definition}
1763	\begin{proof}
1764	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre}
1765	  Construct \(\delta_{I,J}\) as the tensor-product lift of the two commuting
1766	  factors: the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\) on the left
1767	  factor, and the pre-localisation transition hom \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\)
1768	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) on the right factor. Since both land in the
1769	  commutative ring \(R^I_J\), the universal property of the tensor product of
1770	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras yields a unique \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism out
1771	  of \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) restricting to these
1772	  factors; by construction its right component is \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\).
1773	\end{proof}
1774	
1775	\begin{lemma}
1776	[Left component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1777	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left}
1778	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_left}
1779	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1780	  For \(a \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1781	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the left factor by the structure map:
1782	  \[
1783	    \delta_{I,J}(a \otimes 1) \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a),
1784	  \]
1785	  the image of \(a\) under \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] = R^I \to R^I_J\).
1786	\end{lemma}
1787	\begin{proof}
1788	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1789	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1790	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a left elementary tensor \(a \otimes
1791	  1\) is the left factor evaluated at \(a\), i.e.\ the structure map.
1792	\end{proof}
1793	
1794	\begin{lemma}
1795	[Right component of \(\delta_{I,J}\)]
1796	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right}
1797	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_right}
1798	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1799	  For \(b \in \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\), the comorphism \(\delta_{I,J}\)
1800	  (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) acts on the right factor by the pre-localisation
1801	  transition hom:
1802	  \[
1803	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes b) \;=\; \tilde\theta_{I,J}(b)
1804	  \]
1805	  (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}).
1806	\end{lemma}
1807	\begin{proof}
1808	  \uses{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}
1809	  Immediate from the defining tensor-product lift of
1810	  \cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}: the value on a right elementary tensor \(1 \otimes
1811	  b\) is the right factor \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) evaluated at \(b\).
1812	\end{proof}
1813	
1814	\begin{lemma}
1815	[The restricted-diagonal comorphism is surjective]
1816	  \label{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1817	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.diagonalRingMap_surjective}
1818	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1819	  The comorphism \(\delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}
1820	  \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R^I_J\) (\cref{def:gr_diagonalRingMap}) is surjective.
1821	\end{lemma}
1822	\begin{proof}
1823	  \uses{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left, lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
1824	  Let \(z \in R^I_J\). Since \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) is the
1825	  localisation of \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) away from \(P^I_J\), surjectivity of
1826	  the localisation map provides \(a \in R^I\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) with
1827	  \[
1828	    z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \;=\; \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a).
1829	  \]
1830	  Consider the witness \(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]
1831	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]\). Using multiplicativity together with
1832	  \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_left} and \cref{lem:gr_diagonalRingMap_right},
1833	  \[
1834	    \delta_{I,J}\bigl(a \otimes (P^J_I)^n\bigr)
1835	      = \mathrm{alg}_{R^I \to R^I_J}(a) \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1836	      = z \cdot (P^I_J)^n \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)^n
1837	      = z \cdot \bigl(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I)\bigr)^n
1838	      = z,
1839	  \]
1840	  where the last equality uses \(P^I_J \cdot \tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1\) from
1841	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}. Hence \(z\) lies in the image
1842	  of \(\delta_{I,J}\), so \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective.
1843	\end{proof}

1845	\begin{definition}
1846	[Source pullback iso \(e_2\) for the diagonal computation]
1847	  \label{def:gr_pullbackιIso}
1848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.pullbackιIso}
1849	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1850	  The \emph{source pullback iso} \(e_2\) identifies the categorical fibre product
1851	  of two chart inclusions of the glued scheme (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) with
1852	  the overlap chart:
1853	  \[
1854	    e_2 : \mathrm{pullback}\,(\iota_i)\,(\iota_j) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1855	    U^I \cap U^J \;=\; \texttt{chartOverlap}\,(d,r,i,j)
1856	  \]
1857	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_overlap}), where \(\iota_i, \iota_j\) are the open
1858	  immersions of the charts \(U^I, U^J\) into \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1859	\end{definition}
1860	\begin{proof}
1861	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_chart_overlap}
1862	  The glue data of \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} supplies, for each pair \((i,j)\), a
1863	  pullback cone over \(\iota_i, \iota_j\) with apex the overlap chart \(U^I \cap
1864	  U^J\), and this cone is a limit cone. Comparing it with the categorical
1865	  pullback limit cone via the uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism of limit cone points
1866	  yields the desired isomorphism \(e_2\).
1867	\end{proof}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).

547	\begin{lemma}
548	\label{lemma-spec}
549	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
550	The functor $F$ is representable by a scheme.
551	\end{lemma}
552	
553	\begin{proof}
554	We are going to use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-glue-functors}.
555	
556	\medskip\noindent
557	First we check that $F$ satisfies the sheaf property for the
558	Zariski topology. Namely, suppose that $T$ is a scheme,
559	that $T = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ is an open covering,
560	and that $(f_i, \varphi_i) \in F(U_i)$ such that
561	$(f_i, \varphi_i)|_{U_i \cap U_j} = (f_j, \varphi_j)|_{U_i \cap U_j}$.
562	This implies that the morphisms $f_i : U_i \to S$
563	glue to a morphism of schemes $f : T \to S$ such that
564	$f|_{U_i} = f_i$, see Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-glueing-schemes}.
565	Thus $f_i^*\mathcal{A} = f^*\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}$ and by assumption the
566	morphisms $\varphi_i$ agree on $U_i \cap U_j$. Hence by Sheaves,
567	Section \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} these glue to a
568	morphism of $\mathcal{O}_T$-algebras $f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T$.
569	This proves that $F$ satisfies the sheaf condition with respect to
570	the Zariski topology.
571	
572	\medskip\noindent
573	Let $S = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$ be an affine open covering.
574	Let $F_i \subset F$ be the subfunctor consisting of
575	those pairs $(f : T \to S, \varphi)$ such that
576	$f(T) \subset U_i$.
577	
578	\medskip\noindent
579	We have to show each $F_i$ is representable.
580	This is the case because $F_i$ is identified with
581	the functor associated to $U_i$ equipped with
582	the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U_i}$-algebra $\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}$,
583	by Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-base-change}.
584	Thus the result follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-spec-affine}.
585	
586	\medskip\noindent
587	Next we show that $F_i \subset F$ is representable by open immersions.
588	Let $(f : T \to S, \varphi) \in F(T)$. Consider $V_i = f^{-1}(U_i)$.
589	It follows from the definition of $F_i$ that given $a : T' \to T$
590	we gave $a^*(f, \varphi) \in F_i(T')$ if and only if $a(T') \subset V_i$.
591	This is what we were required to show.
592	
593	\medskip\noindent
594	Finally, we have to show that the collection $(F_i)_{i \in I}$
595	covers $F$. Let $(f : T \to S, \varphi) \in F(T)$.
596	Consider $V_i = f^{-1}(U_i)$. Since $S = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i$
597	is an open covering of $S$ we see that $T = \bigcup_{i \in I} V_i$
598	is an open covering of $T$. Moreover $(f, \varphi)|_{V_i} \in F_i(V_i)$.
599	This finishes the proof of the lemma.
600	\end{proof}
601	
602	\begin{lemma}
603	\label{lemma-glueing-gives-functor-spec}
604	In Situation \ref{situation-relative-spec}.
605	The scheme $\pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \to S$
606	constructed in Lemma \ref{lemma-glue-relative-spec}
607	and the scheme representing the functor $F$ are
608	canonically isomorphic as schemes over $S$.
609	\end{lemma}

files = [

for fn in files:

                # print following lines

96	\begin{proof}
97	
98	  \textbf{Primary route — the finite \(A\)-module case.}
99	  Suppose first that \(M\) is module-finite over \(A\) itself. Since \(A\) is noetherian,
100	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). The generic fibre \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\)
101	  (where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\)) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free
102	  over \(K\); by \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} this freeness descends to a single basic open:
103	  there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\). This closes the
104	  theorem whenever \(M\) is finite over \(A\).
105	
106	  \textbf{Surviving residue — reducing the finite-type \(B\)-algebra case to the
107	  finite \(A\)-module case.} The genuine content not supplied by Mathlib is the
108	  reduction of the general hypothesis (\(M\) finite over a \emph{finite-type}
109	  \(A\)-algebra \(B\)) to the finite \(A\)-module situation handled above. Concretely,
110	  the missing piece is the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness: a finite module
111	  over \(A[b_1, \dots, b_n]\) becomes free over \(A_f\) after inverting a single
112	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\). The classical \S4 dévissage below is the fallback route
113	  for exactly this residue.
114	
115	  \emph{Fallback (Nitsure \S4 induction).}
116	  The \S4 dévissage is assembled from the sub-lemmas of
117	  \cref{sec:gf_devissage_chain}. If \(M_K = 0\), the torsion base case
118	  \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} gives \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f = 0\) free. Otherwise the
119	  prime-filtration induction principle \cref{lem:noeth_prime_filtration} over the
120	  noetherian ring \(B\), combined with the splicing fact
121	  \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact}, reduces to \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) and hence to
122	  \(B\) a domain, \(M = B\); Noether normalisation with clearing of denominators
123	  \cref{lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators} makes \(B_g\) finite over a polynomial
124	  ring \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), and the polynomial-ring core
125	  \cref{lem:gf_polynomial_core} — bottoming out at the finite-\(A\)-module leaf
126	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} of the primary route — produces the required
127	  \(f \ne 0\) with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\).
128	\end{proof}

131	\label{sec:gf_devissage_chain}
132

135	finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\) but not module-finite over \(A\) — into the
136	classical \cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}~\S4 induction. They are ordered so that each block
137	depends only on earlier blocks or Mathlib lemmas. Throughout,
138	\(A\) is a noetherian domain with fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\), \(B\) a
139	finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module with the compatible
140	\(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower \(A \to B \to M\).

142	\begin{lemma}[Finite-module leaf — finite \(A\)-module case]
143	  \label{lem:gf_finite_module}
144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite}
145	  \uses{lem:fp_free_descent}
146	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there
147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
148	\end{lemma}
149	
150	\begin{proof}
151	
152	  This is the \(d = 0\) leaf of the induction. A finite module over a noetherian ring is
153	  finitely presented, so
154	  \(M\) is finitely presented over \(A\). Over the generic point
155	  \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A) = S^{-1}A\) (\(S = A \setminus \{0\}\)) the localisation
156	  \(M_K = S^{-1}M\) is a finite-dimensional \(K\)-vector space, hence free over
157	  \(K\). The finite-presentation free-descent lemma \cref{lem:fp_free_descent}
158	  then descends this freeness to a single basic open: there is \(r \in S\), i.e.\
159	  \(r \in A\) with \(r \ne 0\), such that \(M_r\) is free over \(A_r\). Take
160	  \(f := r\).
161	\end{proof}

164	  \label{lem:fp_free_descent}
165	  \lean{Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers}
166	  \mathlibok

168	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative subset, and
169	  \(M\) a finitely-presented \(R\)-module such that the localisation \(M_S\) is
170	  free over the localisation \(R_S\). Then there exists \(r \in S\) such that
171	  \(M_r\) is free over \(R_r\) (of the same finite rank).
172	\end{lemma}

182	  %  each $v\in M$ is annihilated by some non-zero element of $A$. Taking a finite

186	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (base case of the
187	  induction).}
188	  Suppose \(K \otimes_A M = 0\), where \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\). Then there exists
189	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(f M = 0\); consequently \(M_f = 0\) is free over

196	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (base case).}
197	  Since \(K \otimes_A M = S^{-1}M\) with \(S = A \setminus \{0\}\), the hypothesis
198	  \(M_K = 0\) means every element of \(M\) is killed by some non-zero element of
199	  \(A\). Choose a finite \(B\)-generating set \(v_1, \dots, v_k\) of \(M\) (which
200	  exists because \(M\) is a finite \(B\)-module), and for each \(i\) an
201	  \(a_i \in A\), \(a_i \ne 0\), with \(a_i v_i = 0\). The product
202	  \(f := a_1 \cdots a_k\) is non-zero (\(A\) is a domain) and annihilates every
203	  generator, hence \(f M = 0\). Therefore \(M_f = 0\), which is free over \(A_f\).

import re

files = [
    '/home/archon/proj/Quot-Foundations/blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_QuotScheme.tex',

]

# Patterns that should not appear in prose bodies
patterns = [
    r'iter-\d+',

    r'planner.note',
    r'\bby exact\b',
    r'\bby apply\b',
    r'\bby simp\b',

    r'\bby funext\b',
    r'#print axioms',

    r'#eval\b',
    r'set_option backward',
]

    for i, line in enumerate(lines):

        if stripped.startswith('%'):

        # Check for patterns

        if m:
            print(f'{fn}:{i+1}: {line.rstrip()[:100]}')

1230	  coherences (\(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\), \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\)) into a single
1231	  right-adjoint-read isomorphism; composing it with the two affine dictionaries
1232	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, \ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}) yields
1233	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\flat}\). Its agreement with
1234	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read} at the literal projection legs is the cone-leg
1235	  factorization.
1236	\end{proof}
1237	
1238	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1239	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1240	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1241	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1242	
1243	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1244	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1245	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1247	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1248	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1249	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1250	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1251	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1252	\end{lemma}
1253	\begin{proof}

3212	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
3213	
3214	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
3215	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
3216	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
3217	
3218	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3219	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3220	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3221	  \mathlibok
3222	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3223	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3224	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3225	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3226	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3227	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3228	  \[
3229	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3230	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3231	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3232	  \]
3233	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3234	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3235	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3236	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3237	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3238	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3239	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3240	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3241	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3242	\end{lemma}
3243	
3244	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf condition as an equalizer of products]
3245	  \label{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}
3246	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts}
3247	  \mathlibok
3248	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3249	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf with values in a category with products on a
3250	  topological space, and let \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of
3251	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf
3252	  condition on \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if the fork
3253	  \[
3254	    \mathcal{F}(U)
3255	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
3256	    \prod_i \mathcal{F}(U_i)
3257	    \;\underset{\mathrm{rightRes}}{\overset{\mathrm{leftRes}}{\rightrightarrows}}\;
3258	    \prod_{(i,j)} \mathcal{F}(U_i \cap U_j)
3259	  \]
3260	  is a limit (an equalizer), where the two parallel maps restrict the \(U_i\)- and
3261	  \(U_j\)-components to \(U_i \cap U_j\). This is Mathlib's
3262	  \texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafEqualizerProducts} together with the fork
3263	  \texttt{SheafConditionEqualizerProducts.fork}; it is exactly Čech degree
3264	  \(0 \to 1\) and requires no separatedness.
3265	\end{lemma}
3266	
3267	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3268	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3269	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3270	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3271	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3272	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3273	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3274	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3275	  \begin{proof}
3276	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3277	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3278	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3279	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3280	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3281	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3282	  \end{proof}
3283	\end{lemma}
3284	
3285	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3286	[$\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})$ as a finite sheaf-condition equalizer]
3287	  \label{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3288	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork}
3289	  % NOTE (iter-033): the pinned Lean decl is strictly more general than this prose — it takes ANY
3290	  % open family `U : ι → X.Opens` over ANY scheme (no QCQS / finite / affine hypotheses), giving
3291	  % `IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U)`. The finite affine cover of a QCQS scheme is the intended
3292	  % application; the consolidated existential `Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork`
3293	  % now has its own block, Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}.
3294	  \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3295	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of
3296	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Fix a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{1 \le i \le t}\)
3297	  of \(X\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}. Then the module of global
3298	  sections is the equalizer of the two restriction maps of the cover,
3299	  \[
3300	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3301	    \;=\;
3302	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3303	      \textstyle\prod_{i} \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
3304	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
3305	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
3306	    \Bigr),
3307	    \qquad
3308	    s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}}), \quad s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}}),
3309	  \]
3310	  and the products are \emph{finite} (indexed by the finite sets \(\{i\}\) and
3311	  \(\{(i,j)\}\)).
3312	  \begin{proof}
3313	    \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3314	    Apply the equalizer-products form of the sheaf condition
3315	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}) to the underlying sheaf of
3316	    \(\mathcal{F}\) and the cover \(\mathcal{U}\): since \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\), the
3317	    fork displayed there computes \(\mathcal{F}(X) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) as the
3318	    equalizer of \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3319	    \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\). The cover is finite by
3320	    Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}, so both products are finite. The
3321	    module structure is inherited objectwise: the forgetful functor to abelian
3322	    groups preserves and reflects the limit, so the equalizer is computed in
3323	    \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) over the ground ring.
3324	  \end{proof}
3325	\end{lemma}
3326	
3327	\begin{lemma}[Global sections as a finite equalizer over a finite affine cover]
3328	  \label{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}
3329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork}
3330	  \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3331	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme and \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\). Then there
3332	  \emph{exists} a finite affine open cover \(\{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) of \(X\) — \(\iota\) finite, each
3333	  \(U_i\) affine, \(\bigsqcup_i U_i = \top\), all pairwise intersections \(U_i \cap U_j\)
3334	  quasi-compact — whose sheaf-condition fork of the underlying presheaf of \(M\) is a limit:
3335	  \[
3336	    \Gamma(X, M) \;=\; \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3337	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M) \;\rightrightarrows\; \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, M)
3338	    \Bigr).
3339	  \]
3340	  This consolidates the finite affine cover Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs} with the
3341	  sheaf-condition equalizer Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} into a single existential, the
3342	  directly-usable ``global sections as a finite equalizer'' input of the flat-base-change argument.
3343	  \begin{proof}
3344	    \uses{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs, lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3345	    Extract a finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps by
3346	    Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}, and apply the sheaf-condition equalizer
3347	    Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} (in its form valid for any open family) to that cover; the
3348	    pair witnesses the existential.
3349	  \end{proof}
3350	\end{lemma}

3352	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear equalizer presentation (FBC-B build-ahead)}
3353	
3354	The separated-case flat base change Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated} can be organised by

3363	\begin{lemma}[$A$-module structure on cover sections by restriction of scalars]
3364	  \label{lem:gamma_amodule_restriction}
3365	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaCoverRestrictScalars}
3366	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}

3368	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)\) for the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf. For a finite

3373	  \(M\) along inclusions of opens are \(\Gamma(X,-)\)-semilinear over those restriction ring maps,

3378	    the ring map \(A \to \Gamma(U_i,\mathcal{O}_X)\) gives the \(A\)-module structure, and likewise on
3379	    \(U_{ij}\). A presheaf restriction of \(M\) over an inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) is additive and

3386	\begin{lemma}[$A$-linear product restriction maps of the cover]
3387	  \label{lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}
3388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaCoverResMapsALinear}
3389	  \uses{lem:gamma_amodule_restriction, lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}

3391	  the sheaf-condition fork,
3392	  \[
3393	    \mathrm{leftRes},\ \mathrm{rightRes} :

3395	  \]

3402	    \(M\) along an inclusion \(U_{ij} \subseteq U_i\) (resp.\ \(U_{ij} \subseteq U_j\)), which is

3408	\begin{lemma}[$\Gamma(X,M)$ as an $A$-linear equalizer locus]
3409	  \label{lem:gamma_eqLocus_iso}
3410	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaEqLocusIso}
3411	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover, lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}
3412	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_alinear_res_maps}, there is an \(A\)-linear isomorphism
3413	  \[

3415	      \;\subseteq\; \prod_i \Gamma(U_i, M),

3418	  Combined with the base-change-of-equalizer equivalence
3419	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}, this presents flat base change of
3420	  \(\Gamma(X,M)\) as \(- \otimes_A B\) applied to the locus.
3421	  \begin{proof}

3423	    By Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover} the global sections \(\Gamma(X,M)\) are the limit
3424	    of the sheaf-condition fork in abelian groups; the limit of a parallel pair is the equalizer,
3425	    whose underlying set is \(\{s : \mathrm{leftRes}\,s = \mathrm{rightRes}\,s\}\), i.e.\ the set-level
3426	    locus \(\operatorname{eqLocus}(\mathrm{leftRes},\mathrm{rightRes})\). The forgetful functor

3433	\begin{lemma}[Base change of the finite equalizer diagram]
3434	  \label{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}

3436	  \uses{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3437	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH ($i=0$ case), separated paragraph
3438	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.md; quoted verbatim in the
3439	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF of thm:flat_base_change_pushforward below).
3440	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$
3441	  % we obtain by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$
3442	  % is affine and that $X_B$ is separated. ... We have the following relation
3443	  % between the {\v C}ech complexes
3444	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
3445	  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B$
3446	  % as follows from Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}."
3447	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), $i=0$ case,
3448	  the relation \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B,\mathcal{F}_B) =
3449	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},\mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B\).}

3452	  \(\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent. Set \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec} A}
3453	  \operatorname{Spec} B\), \(\mathcal{F}_B\) the pullback, and
3454	  \(\mathcal{U}_B = \{(U_i)_B\}\). Then \(\mathcal{U}_B\) is a finite affine cover of
3455	  \(X_B\), and the sheaf-condition fork of Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer} for
3456	  \((X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) is obtained from that for \((X, \mathcal{F})\) by applying
3457	  \(- \otimes_A B\): there are compatible isomorphisms
3458	  \[
3459	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3460	    \qquad
3461	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3462	  \]
3463	  intertwining the two restriction maps, so that the whole diagram
3464	  \(\prod_i \Gamma((U_i)_B,\mathcal{F}_B) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3465	  \Gamma((U_{ij})_B,\mathcal{F}_B)\) is \((- \otimes_A B)\) applied to

3471	    \(\{(U_i)_B\}\) covers \(X_B\); since \(X\) is separated each \(U_{ij}\), hence each
3472	    \((U_{ij})_B\), is affine, and the fork of

3474	    affine term the global-sections form of the affine lemma
3475	    Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} supplies the isomorphism

3485	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change, separated case]
